title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a coach called Jade talking about a sports volunteering programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a coach called Jade talking about a sports volunteering programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A coach called Jade explains a sports volunteering programme.
extracts.audio_script: Hi. I’m Jade and I run Active Neighbours, a scheme that trains volunteers to assist at low-cost sports sessions for children. We work in an area where many families want activities close to home. Our original sessions were held outdoors, but rain caused too many cancellations. We now use a converted [9]factory building[/9], with a sports hall downstairs and small training rooms upstairs. The outside still looks industrial, but inside it is bright, safe and large enough for groups to move around freely.||The scheme offers football, basketball, dance and general games for children aged eight to thirteen. Volunteers may have a favourite sport, but they are trained to support different activities. This term the sessions attracting the greatest number of children are in [10]basketball[/10], partly because a local team has recently been successful. At the first volunteer meeting, we do not discuss winning games or teaching complicated skills. The main subject is [11]child protection[/11], including clear rules about behaviour, communication and reporting concerns to staff. All sessions are supervised by qualified coaches.||People do not need their own sports equipment. We provide balls, cones and brightly coloured bibs, while volunteers should arrive in comfortable sportswear and bring a [12]whistle[/12], which is useful when supporting simple activities and moving groups safely between stations. New helpers initially avoid leading games alone. Their first role is usually organising [13]warm-up activities[/13], copying a prepared plan with an experienced coach nearby. This gives them a chance to learn children's names and practise giving clear instructions without needing advanced coaching knowledge.||We encourage inclusion, and every session includes alternatives for children with different abilities. We have recently purchased lighter balls and adjustable targets. Next month, volunteers will attend a specialist workshop about [14]inclusive games[/14], showing how rules can be changed so everyone can participate. The scheme also offers a holiday activity day. Last summer it focused on team races. This year children voted most strongly for a [15]dance festival[/15], where groups will learn short routines and perform them informally for families at the end of the day.||Volunteers who attend regularly can benefit from the scheme. After supporting twenty sessions, people aged sixteen or over may take a free [16]coaching course[/16], which is the first step towards more formal training. Every volunteer is also provided with a [17]sports shirt[/17] bearing the scheme's name, so families know who can help during events. Finally, we want to offer outdoor activities in summer, but the playground beside our building needs improvement. Money from the family fun day will pay for new [18]sports markings[/18] on the surface, including courts and activity zones. Registration forms are available tonight.

dialogue.speaker: jade
dialogue.text: Hi. I’m Jade and I run Active Neighbours, a scheme that trains volunteers to assist at low-cost sports sessions for children. We work in an area where many families want activities close to home. Our original sessions were held outdoors, but rain caused too many cancellations. We now use a converted factory building, with a sports hall downstairs and small training rooms upstairs. The outside still looks industrial, but inside it is bright, safe and large enough for groups to move around freely.||The scheme offers football, basketball, dance and general games for children aged eight to thirteen. Volunteers may have a favourite sport, but they are trained to support different activities. This term the sessions attracting the greatest number of children are in basketball, partly because a local team has recently been successful. At the first volunteer meeting, we do not discuss winning games or teaching complicated skills. The main subject is child protection, including clear rules about behaviour, communication and reporting concerns to staff. All sessions are supervised by qualified coaches.||People do not need their own sports equipment. We provide balls, cones and brightly coloured bibs, while volunteers should arrive in comfortable sportswear and bring a whistle, which is useful when supporting simple activities and moving groups safely between stations. New helpers initially avoid leading games alone. Their first role is usually organising warm-up activities, copying a prepared plan with an experienced coach nearby. This gives them a chance to learn children's names and practise giving clear instructions without needing advanced coaching knowledge.||We encourage inclusion, and every session includes alternatives for children with different abilities. We have recently purchased lighter balls and adjustable targets. Next month, volunteers will attend a specialist workshop about inclusive games, showing how rules can be changed so everyone can participate. The scheme also offers a holiday activity day. Last summer it focused on team races. This year children voted most strongly for a dance festival, where groups will learn short routines and perform them informally for families at the end of the day.||Volunteers who attend regularly can benefit from the scheme. After supporting twenty sessions, people aged sixteen or over may take a free coaching course, which is the first step towards more formal training. Every volunteer is also provided with a sports shirt bearing the scheme's name, so families know who can help during events. Finally, we want to offer outdoor activities in summer, but the playground beside our building needs improvement. Money from the family fun day will pay for new sports markings on the surface, including courts and activity zones. Registration forms are available tonight.
